title: The Anger of Adoptees
dek: *The fury of being given away lives in the bones of adoptees, a wordless howl that predates memory. It hides behind gratitude, politeness, and the fear of seeming ungrateful. Yet until this anger is met with compassion, the body holds it like a clenched fist that never opens.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Adoptees carry a unique form of anger rooted in preverbal separation, often invisible even to themselves. This rage lives in the body's tissues and nervous system, erupting sideways or turning inward as shame. Recognition and safe practice can transform this old fire into vitality and self-trust.

She sat across from her adoptive mother at brunch, fork hovering over eggs Benedict, listening to the familiar refrain about how lucky she was. Her chest tightened. Her throat closed. She smiled wider, nodded, changed the subject to her mother's garden. Later, alone in her car, she gripped the steering wheel until her knuckles went white, but she couldn't name what she felt. It wasn't anger. It couldn't be. Anger would make her a monster. ## The Invisible Wound That Precedes Words Adoption begins with loss. Before language, before the capacity to understand or contextualize, an infant loses the heartbeat they knew in utero, the smell of the person who carried them, the voice that vibrated through water and bone for nine months. This is not a cognitive event. It's a nervous system event, what trauma researcher Peter Levine describes as an overwhelming experience the body cannot complete or discharge. The anger that arises from this primal separation often remains invisible because it forms before the brain develops the structures for explicit memory. You can't remember what happened, so how can you be angry about it? Yet the body remembers. The autonomic nervous system remembers. Research documented in the NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system shows that unresolved anger creates persistent patterns of sympathetic activation, a state of perpetual readiness for threat that many adoptees carry without conscious awareness. In my own practice, I've noticed that adoptees often describe feeling "on edge" or "ready to run" without knowing why. This isn't pathology. It's an intelligent response to an impossible situation: the person you needed most disappeared, and your nervous system organized itself around that fact. ## Gratitude as a Gag Order Adoptees grow up hearing they should be grateful. Grateful to be chosen. Grateful to be saved. Grateful for the opportunity their birth parents couldn't provide. This narrative, however well-intentioned, functions as a silencer. How can you be angry when you're supposed to be thankful? Psychotherapist Beverly Engel has written extensively about how imposed gratitude creates what she calls "forbidden anger", emotions that feel too dangerous to acknowledge because they threaten the very relationships an adoptee depends on for survival. A child who has already lost one set of parents cannot risk losing another by expressing rage. So the anger goes underground. It becomes perfectionism. It becomes people-pleasing. It becomes a hair-trigger sensitivity to rejection or a complete numbing to all emotion. The adoptee learns to perform gratitude while the body stores fury in the jaw, the shoulders, the gut. You might find yourself clenching your teeth at night, waking with headaches, or developing digestive issues that no doctor can fully explain. These aren't random symptoms. They're the body speaking what the mouth cannot. Over the years I've seen adoptees who can advocate fiercely for others but go silent when their own boundaries are crossed. The anger that should protect the self gets redirected outward or turned completely inward, becoming depression or self-harm. ## The Sideways Eruption Because direct anger feels too dangerous, it often emerges sideways. You find yourself enraged at a stranger who cut you off in traffic, or sobbing over a minor disappointment, or picking fights with your partner about dishes in the sink. The actual source, the original abandonment, remains untouched because touching it feels like opening a door to annihilation. Gabor Maté points out in his work on trauma and addiction that anger is one of the most suppressed emotions in our culture, particularly for those who experienced early disruption in attachment. The child who cannot afford to be angry at their caregivers (because they need those caregivers to survive) learns to redirect that anger in ways that won't threaten the attachment bond. For adoptees, this pattern can continue into adulthood long after the original threat has passed. You're no longer a helpless infant dependent on your adoptive parents' goodwill, but your nervous system hasn't received that update. It still believes that your anger could cause the people you love to leave, so it finds other outlets. Some adoptees describe sudden explosive rage that seems to come from nowhere, a volcanic eruption after years of pressure. Others describe a constant low-grade irritability, a baseline state of friction with the world. The sideways anger often targets safe people, those who won't actually leave: long-suffering partners, close friends, or yourself. It's misdirection, but it's not conscious deception. It's the psyche's attempt to metabolize an overwhelming emotion through whatever channels feel survivable. ## Anger Turned Inward Becomes Shame When anger cannot go out, it goes in. For many adoptees, rage at being given away transforms into a deep conviction that something must be fundamentally wrong with them. Why else would they have been relinquished? This is the birthplace of toxic shame, the belief that you are inherently defective, unworthy, too much or not enough. Shame and anger are intimately connected. Shame says, "I am bad." Anger says, "I was wronged." For an adoptee, allowing the anger means confronting the reality that the people who were supposed to protect you didn't, or couldn't. It means acknowledging that your origin story includes abandonment, and that's a truth that can feel unbearable. So instead, the anger curls inward and becomes self-attack. You develop an inner critic with a voice like broken glass. You sabotage relationships before they can reject you. You push yourself relentlessly, trying to earn the love that should have been your birthright. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the way unexpressed anger calcifies into self-hatred when it has nowhere else to go. Your body might express this inward-turned rage as chronic pain, autoimmune conditions, or what some practitioners call "self-attack" patterns where the immune system treats the self as a threat. The anger is there. It's just aimed in the wrong direction. ## The Body Keeps the Tally Your muscles remember what your mind has forgotten. Levine's work on somatic experiencing emphasizes that trauma lives in the body as incomplete defensive responses, frozen fight-or-flight reactions that never got to finish. For adoptees, the original separation triggered a massive stress response in an infant body that had no capacity to fight or flee. That energy is still there, locked in tissue, waiting for completion. You might notice it as chronic tension in your shoulders, as if you're perpetually bracing for impact. Or as a tight throat, the physical manifestation of words you never got to speak. Some adoptees describe a feeling of "buzzing" or restlessness in their limbs, an urge to run that has no destination. These are not metaphors. They're the literal activation patterns of a nervous system that went into survival mode and never fully came out. When you're sitting in a staff meeting and your heart starts racing for no apparent reason, that's not anxiety arriving out of nowhere. That's your body responding to a perceived threat, probably something about the social dynamics or power structure that echoes the original wound. Your conscious mind might not make the connection, but your autonomic nervous system is exquisitely attuned to cues of potential abandonment or rejection. Working with this kind of body-held anger requires more than cognitive understanding. You can know intellectually that you have a right to be angry, but if your nervous system is still organized around the belief that anger equals abandonment, the knowing won't change the feeling. You need practices that speak the body's language: movement, breath, touch, sound. You need to give that old defensive energy a chance to complete. ## A Practice for Meeting the Anger in Your Body Find a private space where you won't be interrupted for five minutes. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Take a few breaths and notice where you feel tension or holding in your body. Don't try to change it yet. Just notice. Now, with your next exhale, let out a sound. It doesn't have to be loud. It can be a sigh, a hum, a growl. Let the sound carry some of the tension out of your body. Do this three times, allowing each sound to be different, to follow whatever wants to emerge. Next, make fists with your hands and press them together in front of your chest. Press hard, engaging your arms and shoulders. Hold for a count of five while breathing, then release with an audible exhale. Repeat this three times. This gives your body a chance to engage the muscles of defense and protection in a bounded way. Finally, shake. Shake your hands, then your arms, then your whole body if that feels right. Let it be messy and awkward. You're discharging activation, letting the nervous system complete a cycle. When you're done, place both hands on your belly and take three slow breaths. Notice how you feel now compared to when you started. This practice isn't about "getting rid of" anger. It's about creating a relationship with it, giving it small, safe channels of expression so it doesn't have to stay locked in your tissues or explode sideways. ## Allies in the Herbal World Some adoptees find that gentle nervous system support from [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) can create enough softness for anger work to feel approachable. [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Holly (Non-Alcohol Formula) - Goodwill Towards Ot](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B09HH1D3WS?tag=spankyspinola-20) is traditionally used for feelings of hatred, envy, and suspicion, emotions that often mask deeper anger. A few drops under the tongue can help create a little space between you and the intensity of the feeling. For those whose anger manifests as agitation or an inability to settle, [Gaia Herbs PRO Calm Restore](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0BTMTSSZ6?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers adaptogenic support for a nervous system stuck in overdrive. It won't suppress your anger, but it might help you access it without feeling flooded. In Chinese medicine, some patterns of anger relate to kidney qi deficiency, particularly when the anger is accompanied by deep fatigue or a sense of depletion. [Plum Flower You Gui Wan (200 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004ZH5YIW?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a classical formula for warming and tonifying kidney yang, supporting the foundational energy that allows us to meet difficult emotions without collapsing. These aren't solutions. They're supports, gentle allies as you do the harder work of feeling what you've spent a lifetime not feeling. ## The Anger That Protects Here's what many adoptees discover when they finally allow themselves to feel their anger: it's not the monster they feared. It's a protector. Anger, in its clean form, is the emotion that says "this is not okay" and "I matter." It's the force that sets boundaries, that says no, that insists on your right to take up space in the world. When you've been taught that your existence is contingent on other people's choices, anger becomes the energy that reclaims your sovereignty. It says, "I didn't choose this, and I'm allowed to have feelings about it." It says, "I can be grateful for my adoptive family and still grieve what I lost." It holds complexity without collapsing into either-or thinking. In our experience, the adoptees who do this work, who turn toward their anger with curiosity instead of fear, often discover a vitality they didn't know they had access to. The energy that was locked up in suppression becomes available for creativity, for boundary-setting, for authentic relationship. The chronic fatigue lifts. The people-pleasing softens. There's a solidity to the self that wasn't there before. This doesn't mean the anger goes away completely. It means you develop a relationship with it, an ability to feel it, express it appropriately, and let it move through you instead of taking up permanent residence in your body. ## Common Questions About Adoptee Anger ### Isn't it harmful to focus on anger when I should be healing? Healing doesn't happen by bypassing difficult emotions. It happens by moving through them. Focusing on your anger isn't dwelling in negativity; it's acknowledging a truth your body already knows. The harm comes from continued suppression, which keeps you disconnected from your full emotional range and your authentic self. Anger, when met with compassion, becomes a doorway to deeper healing, not an obstacle to it. ### What if expressing my anger damages my relationship with my adoptive parents? This is a legitimate fear, and it requires nuance. Expressing anger doesn't necessarily mean confronting your adoptive parents directly. It means first acknowledging the anger to yourself, feeling it in your body, working with it in therapy or trusted relationships. You can be angry about what happened without making your adoptive parents responsible for healing that anger. Many adoptees find that once they've processed their anger privately, their relationships with their adoptive families actually improve because they're no longer performing gratitude or suppressing resentment. ### How do I know if my anger is "adoptee anger" or just regular anger about current situations? Often it's both. Current situations can activate the old wound, and that's actually information. If your anger feels disproportionate to the present trigger, if it has a quality of desperation or existential threat, if it's accompanied by younger feelings like helplessness or terror, you're probably touching the original anger. Working with a therapist familiar with adoption trauma can help you track these layers and give each its due attention. ## Opening the Clenched Fist The work of meeting adoptee anger is not a one-time event. It's a practice, a gradual softening of defenses that have been necessary for survival. Some days you'll be able to feel it cleanly, to name it, to let it move through you. Other days you'll find yourself back in the old patterns, smiling when you want to scream, saying "I'm fine" when you're not. This is not failure. This is the nature of deep work. You're rewiring neural pathways that formed before you had language, renegotiating a contract with your nervous system that was written in your first hours of life. Be patient with yourself. The anger has waited this long. It will wait while you learn how to hold it. What shifts, over time, is your capacity to be with the intensity without it destroying you. You discover that you can feel furious and still be safe. You can express anger and still be loved. You can acknowledge what was lost without losing what you have. The clenched fist begins to open, and in that opening is not emptiness but power, the power of a person who knows their own truth and isn't afraid to speak it. For more resources on emotional alchemy and body-centered practices, visit [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) or explore [more articles in this journal](/articles). You can learn about the perspective and experience informing this work on [the about page](/about). The anger is not the enemy. It never was. It's been waiting all along to show you the way home to yourself. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on the anger of adoptees, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
